coredata.dc:title: Two-dimensional TiO2-based nanosheets co-modified by surface-enriched carbon dots and Gd2O3 nanoparticles for efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Dingze
Fang, Pengfei
Ding, Junqian
Yang, Minchen
Cao, Yufei
Zhou, Yawei
Peng, Kui
Kondamareddy, Kiran Kumar
Liu, Min
coredata.dc:description: Two-dimensional TiO2-based nanosheets (TNSs) co-modified by surface-enriched carbon dots (CDs) and Gd2O3 nanoparticles: (Gd-C-TNSs), capable of exhibiting visible-light-driven photo catalysis were synthesized using a two-pot hydrothermal route. The samples had a sheet-like structure, thickness of approximately 3.6nm, large specific surface area of 240–350cm2/g. The CDs (2–3nm) and Gd2O3 nanoparticles (1–2nm) were highly dispersed over the surface of the nanosheets. The co-modification by Gd2O3 nanoparticles and CDs influenced the crystallinity, crystal structure, and surface area of the TNSs, and improved the visible-light absorption. Surface photocurrent and fluorescence spectral studies revealed that the photo-generated charge carrier separation efficiency could be improved by an appropriate amount of modification. A very high efficiency was obtained using 0.5 at% Gd/Ti and 3.0g/L of CDs. The visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity is enhanced under the isolated Cr(VI) system, isolated Rhodamin B (RhB) system, and the synergism between RhB degradation and Cr(VI) reduction for the Gd-C-TNSs photocatalysts. Initially, the photocatalytic activity gradually increased with an increase in the amount of CDs, and then decreased after attaining a maximum, in the case where 0.5 at% Gd/Ti and 3.0g/L of CDs were used. The enhancement in the photocatalytic activity was attributed to the synergetic effect of the Gd2O3 nanoparticles, TNSs, and CDs in the Gd-C-TNSs composites. The effect led to a fast separation and slow recombination of photo-induced electron–hole pairs. An alternate mechanism for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity was also considered.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of surface-enriched Gd2O3 nanoparticle and carbon dot co-modified TiO2-based nanosheets synthesized using novel two-pot hydrothermal method.
(a) Crystal structure diagrams of P25 (anatase), Na2Ti3O7 along [001] axis, and H2Ti3O7 along [001] axis, and (b) evaluation of two different preparation processes for Gd-C-TNSs.

Schematic diagram for charge separation and transfer of Gd-C-TNS photocatalysts with surface-enriched Gd2O3 nanoparticle and carbon dot co-modified TiO2-based nanosheets in process of simultaneous Cr(VI) and RhB decontamination in Cr(VI)–RhB coexistence system under visible light irradiation.
TEM images: partial SAED patterns and HRTEM images of (a–b) P25, (c–d) TNSs, (e–f) 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, (g–h) C-3-TNSs, and (i–j) 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs, respectively.

(a) XRD patterns of P25, TNSs, and @Gd-C-0-TNSs (@=0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%, and 2.0%); (b) XRD patterns of 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0); (c) Raman spectra of P25, TNSs, and @Gd-C-0-TNSs (@=0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%, and 2.0%); (d) Raman spectra of 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0).

XPS spectra: (a) survey spectrum; (b) O 1s region of TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs; (c) Ti 2p region of TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs; (d) Gd 4d region of 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs; (e) C 1s region of 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) fluorescence spectra, (c) surface photocurrent curves, and (d) electrochemical impedance spectra of P25, TNSs, and as-prepared 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0).

Visible-light-induced photocatalytic performance evaluations: (a) photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) using 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNS sample, (b) photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) in various samples, (c) visible-light-induced reaction kinetics (ln (C t/C 0)=−K app t), and (d) reaction rate constant (K app) comparison of various samples. The initial concentration of Cr(VI) was 50ppm, and the solution pH was 3.0.

Visible-light-induced photocatalytic performance evaluation: (a) photodegradation of RhB using 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs, (b) photocatalytic degradation of RhB, (c) reaction kinetics (ln (C/C 0)=−K app t) of photoreaction, and (d) reaction rate constant values (K app). The initial concentration of RhB was 20ppm, and the solution pH was 7.0.

(a) Kinetics of RhB degradation in absence or presence of Cr(VI). (b) Kinetics of Cr(VI) reduction in absence or presence of RhB. (c) Removal efficiencies for total Cr, Cr(VI), and Cr(III) in Cr(VI)–RhB coexistence system. (d) The change in TOC in single RhB system and Cr(VI)-RhB coexistence system. The initial concentrations of Cr(VI) and RhB were 50ppm and at 20ppm, respectively. The dosage of 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs was 1gL−1, and the solution pH was 3.0.

(a) Formation of 7-hydroxycoumarin (monitored by fluorescence emission) in suspensions of TNSs and Gd-C-TNSs. (b) The formation rate constants of 7-hydroxycoumarin over various samples (columns a–h: P25, TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-1-TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-2-TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-6-TNSs, and 0.5%Gd-C-10-TNSs, respectively).
(a) Recycle usability of 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs in Cr(VI) or RhB system. (b) The reuse of 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs in Cr(VI)–RhB coexisting system.
Structural parameters of P25, unloaded TNSs, and Gd-C-TNSs composites.

head.ce:title: Two-dimensional TiO2-based nanosheets co-modified by surface-enriched carbon dots and Gd2O3 nanoparticles for efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-dimensional TiO2-based nanosheets (TNSs) co-modified by surface-enriched carbon dots (CDs) and Gd2O3 nanoparticles: (Gd-C-TNSs), capable of exhibiting visible-light-driven photo catalysis were synthesized using a two-pot hydrothermal route. The samples had a sheet-like structure, thickness of approximately 3.6nm, large specific surface area of 240–350cm2/g. The CDs (2–3nm) and Gd2O3 nanoparticles (1–2nm) were highly dispersed over the surface of the nanosheets. The co-modification by Gd2O3 nanoparticles and CDs influenced the crystallinity, crystal structure, and surface area of the TNSs, and improved the visible-light absorption. Surface photocurrent and fluorescence spectral studies revealed that the photo-generated charge carrier separation efficiency could be improved by an appropriate amount of modification. A very high efficiency was obtained using 0.5 at% Gd/Ti and 3.0g/L of CDs. The visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity is enhanced under the isolated Cr(VI) system, isolated Rhodamin B (RhB) system, and the synergism between RhB degradation and Cr(VI) reduction for the Gd-C-TNSs photocatalysts. Initially, the photocatalytic activity gradually increased with an increase in the amount of CDs, and then decreased after attaining a maximum, in the case where 0.5 at% Gd/Ti and 3.0g/L of CDs were used. The enhancement in the photocatalytic activity was attributed to the synergetic effect of the Gd2O3 nanoparticles, TNSs, and CDs in the Gd-C-TNSs composites. The effect led to a fast separation and slow recombination of photo-induced electron–hole pairs. An alternate mechanism for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity was also considered.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Surface enrichment of CDs and Gd2O3 nanoparticles

ce:section.ce:para: Research to eliminate toxic elements from municipal and industrial wastewater has generated much interest because of the growing global demand for clean water. Wastewater can contain several inorganic heavy metals (Cr(VI), Hg(II), Pd(II), Pt(IV), etc.) as well as organic pollutants (phenol, polychlorinated biphenyls, etc.), which can lead to severe aquatic problems as a result of their toxicity, persistence, and bioaccumulation [1–3]. Various physical, chemical, and biological methods are being implemented to simultaneously remove these organic and inorganic pollutants from wastewater. In particular, photocatalytic technology is very promising because of its ability to mineralize a majority of organic pollutants utilizing luminous energy. TiO2 is considered the most extensively utilized and representative photocatalyst because of its non-toxicity, low cost, high chemical stability, high photo-activity, and high oxidation ability [4–7]. Nevertheless, large-scale applications, mainly in the visible region, are limited by the high recombination rate of photo-induced electron–hole pairs and wide band gap (∼3.2eV) of TiO2 [8,9]. With the purpose of fully utilizing materials was decreased to make the absorbing edge enter the visible area of the electromagnetic spectrum, in which the gross irradiance of the Sun is about 42%.

Recently, increasing attention has been given to the development and improvement of two-dimensional (2D) TiO2-based nanomaterials, inspired by the leaves of plants, which facilitate a natural photochemical process that converts sunlight into chemical energy through photosynthesis with high efficiency [10–18] because of their low thickness and high specific surface area. 2D photocatalysts allow large exposure to light because of their high surface area for a more effective photocatalytic reaction and can make connections with a larger number of pollutant molecules. In addition, the separation rate of the charge carriers is improved by the low thickness of the photocatalyst. Various methods have been attempted to prepare nanosheets, including the sol–gel method, hydrothermal method, and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [19–23]. Because of advantages such as convenience and simplicity, the hydrothermal approach has become one of the most widely used methods. Under hydrothermal conditions, TiO2 nanoparticles (3D) adopt a nanosheet (2D) structure and eventually turn into nanotubes (1D). Various nanostructures for TiO2-based nanomaterials can be synthesized by controlling the reaction environment. In a previous work, TiO2-oriented nanosheets (TNSs) were synthesized with a specific surface area (∼339m2/g) and thickness (∼4–5nm). It was reported that the TNSs demonstrated better UV–vis photocatalytic activity than Degussa P25 for the degradation of original pollutants such as crystal violet (CV), methylene blue (MB), and Rhodamine B (RhB) [24,25].

However, because of the high recombination rate of photo-induced carriers, the visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity of pure TNSs is relatively low. To make use of a wider spectral range of solar energy and improve the transfer efficiency of the interfacial charges, the TNSs were modified by doping and manipulating their nanomaterial composition [26,27]. In our previous work [28], various TNS/Gd2O3 complex systems were synthesized using a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach. Furthermore, excellent visible-light photocatalytic abilities were achieved for the degradation of organic molecules (e.g., RhB, MB, CV, and phenol), with an optimum Gd/Ti content of 0.5at.%, even though they showed poor performance for the removal of inorganic ions. In order to further improve the photocatalytic efficiency when removing inorganic ions, modification by carbon dots (CDs) was employed as an important target [29,30]. CDs have been successfully utilized in catalytic applications as a new class of nanocarbons because of their robust chemical inertness, sound size-dependent and up-converting photoluminescence, high aqueous solubility, superior stability against photobleaching, and low toxicity [31–35]. In addition, rare earth gadolinium metals with a half-filled 4f-shell containing only seven electrons and an empty 5d-shell often serve as trapping centers to prevent the recombination of carriers [36,37], and a synergistic reaction may occur for the visible-light photocatalytic activities with the co-modification by Gd2O3 and CDs. Therefore, because of the good photosensitizing efficiency of CDs provided by their narrow energy gap, 2D Gd2O3/TNSs modified with surface-enriched CDs might be an effective approach to obtain excellent visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity. The strong chemical interaction between CDs and 2D TNSs/Gd2O3 may provide an effective charge transfer between them and make the composites more stable, thereby resulting in greater photocatalytic activity. In addition, during the photocatalytic reaction, the unique morphology and structure of the TNSs are important to the determination of the stability and dispersion of the modification against leaching.

The visible-light-active 2D TNSs/Gd2O3 modified by surface-enriched CDs (Gd-C-TNSs) were synthesized using a novel two-pot hydrothermal method in this work. The surface-enriched CDs and Gd2O3 nanoparticles were dispersed on the surface of the TNSs. The simultaneous photocatalytic detoxification of Cr(VI) and RhB degradation were used to evaluate the photocatalytic activities of the Gd-C-TNSs. Cr(VI), which is the most environmentally toxic ion, was chosen as a reference metal ion because it is toxic to a majority of organisms under a concentration of greater than 0.05ppm. Various industries produce wastewater with toxic aromatic compounds such as RhB and their derivatives, which have negative impacts on the environment and health. Hence, RhB was chosen as an oxidizable organic compound, which acted as an electron donor in the photocatalytic reaction for the simultaneous photocatalytic reduction–oxidation system.

All the reagents were used with no further purification because they were analytically pure. Deionized (DI) water (resistance of 4.25MΩcm) was used in all the experiments. A novel two-step hydrothermal method was used to prepare photocatalysts as shown in Scheme 1 . The first step was the synthesis of Gd2O3-loaded sodium titanate nanosheets (Gd-Na-TNSs). First, 0.8g of P25 was diffused in 50mL of a 10mol/L NaOH solution. The mixture was then transferred to a 100mL Teflon reactor. A certain amount of gadolinium nitrate solution was slowly dropped into the suspension. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 130°C for 3h, after which, the samples were washed using DI water multiple times until reaching pH 7. The CD deposition was performed as the second step by adding the samples obtained from the previous stage to 50mL of glucose solution and stirring magnetically for 30min, after which the solution was transferred to a 100mL Teflon-lined autoclave and allowed to react at 180°C for 3h to accomplish the CD deposition. The samples were then washed with DI water multiple times. The samples were proton exchanged by adding them to 200mL of HCl solution (0.1mol/L) to remove the Na+. The mixtures were stirred for 3h. DI water was used to wash the samples again until reaching pH 7 after the proton exchange treatment. Finally, the photocatalysts were heated in an oven for 12h at 75°C. The samples were denoted by @Gd-C-X-TNSs, where @Gd indicates various ratios of Gd/Ti (0.0%, 0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%, and 2.0%) and X indicates different concentrations of glucose (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0g/L). For a comparative study, unloaded TiO2-based nanosheets (TNSs) were synthesized using the same procedure without the addition of the gadolinium and carbon source.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEOL TEM-2010 (HT), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using a JEM-2010 FEM, and field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (FEI, Netherlands and SIRIO) were employed to study the morphological characteristics of the samples. The crystal structure of each of the specimens was characterized using a Bruker D8 advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation. The 2θ value ranged from 20° to 80° with 0.02° steps, and the accelerating current and voltage were set at 40mA and 40kV, respectively. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was utilized to study the thickness and surface morphology of each specimen. A silicon cantilever with a normal spring constant of 16Nm−1 at a 0.4Hz scan speed and a high frequency of 130kHz was utilized to conduct the measurements in a tapping model. The isotherm of nitrogen adsorption–desorption at 77K was used to measure the specific surface areas of the samples utilizing an instrument (JW-BK122W, China). The Raman spectra were obtained using a Horiba Lab RAM HR Raman spectrometer equipped with an excitation wavelength of 448nm. A Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer with a BaSO4-coated integration sphere against a BaSO4 reference and a diffuse reflectance attachment was used to evaluate the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the samples. The chemical compositions of the samples and valence states of the different elements were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi and XPS). The spectra could be studied by conducting a Shirley background subtraction, and the Acq approach could be used by the XPS Peak 4.1 software. Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES; Optima 4300 DV, America) was employed to evaluate the chemical compositions. The Hitachi FL 4600 was used to survey the fluorescence (FL) spectra using a 700V photomultiplier to study the recombination efficiency of the photo-generated holes and electrons.

The photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) was carried out at 25°C employing a 200mL quartz reactor that contained 100mg of photocatalyst and 100mL of potassium dichromate aqueous solution (50ppm). The pH value of the solution was regulated by adding NaOH or HCl solution. The adsorption was monitored in the dark by the period collection of a small amount of solution (0.2mL), followed by dilution, commixture, and evaluation. The reactor was exposed to a 300W Xe lamp after attaining a balance between desorption and adsorption. The entire reaction process was continued for 120min. Meanwhile, 0.8mL of solution was collected every 20min and centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 2min to obtain segregated photocatalyst. The final products were obtained by mixing 2.8mL of DI water and 0.4mL of supernatant liquor. The Cr(VI) content of the supernatant solution was studied colorimetrically (at 540nm) utilizing the diphenylcarbazide approach (DPC) with a UV–vis spectrometer. A cut-off filter (λ≥420nm) was applied to measure the visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of simultaneous photocatalytic decontamination of RhB and Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the photocatalytic activity of specimens for the simultaneous removal of RhB and Cr(VI), experiments were conducted in a 200mL quartz reactor filled with 100mg of photocatalyst and 100mL of an aqueous solution of Cr(VI) ions and RhB at concentrations of 50ppm and 20ppm, respectively. In order to maintain the pH value of the reacting solution at 3.0, HCl was added. The mixtures were stirred for 1h in darkness to ensure that the desorption–adsorption balance was obtained prior to the photocatalytic reaction. The entire reaction process required 70min. In the supernatant solution, the Cr(VI) content was evaluated every 10min as mentioned in the previous section. Based on the Beer–Lambert law, a spectroscopic calibration was applied to quantify the RhB concentration at 554nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface photocurrent (SPC) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 50mg of TNSs or Gd-C-TNSs were dispersed in 5mL of ethanol. After sonication for around 90min, the slurries were dip-coated onto a glass electrode (1×2cm) of indium-tin oxide (ITO) and dried for 5h at 50°C. A platinum electrode, as-prepared ITO/photocatalyst electrode, and saturation calomel electrode were employed in the SPC tests as the counter electrode, working electrode, and reference electrode, respectively, with 0.1M of the Na2SO4 solution used as the electrolyte. The working electrode (the ITO electrode/prepared photocatalyst) was irradiated horizontally, using a high pressure Xe lamp (300W) with a cut-off filter (λ≥420nm). The Xe lamp was placed 15cm from the working electrode. In the present experiments, an electrochemical system (CHI-660C, Chenhua, China) was also used to obtain the EIS, employing a cell configuration similar to that used in the SPC tests, with 0.1M of Na2S utilized as the electrolyte. The visible light was provided by a 300W Xe lamp employing a 420nm cut-off filter.

In order to analyze the formation of OH radicals, the photoluminescence (PL) technique was carried out using coumarin (COU) as a probe molecule, which can readily react with OH to form a highly fluorescent COU-OH adduct (7-hydroxycoumarin) [24,26]. The PL intensity of 7-hydroxycoumarin (7-HC) was proportional to the number of formed OH radicals. The same cut-off filters were adopted to obtain visible light, and 0.2g of photocatalyst was added to the solution of aqueous COU (100mL, 1mM) under continuous stirring. Under constant air-equilibrated conditions, suspensions that contained photocatalyst powder and COU were irradiated using the 300W Xe lamp after being magnetically stirred for 1h in darkness, to obtain an adsorption–desorption balance. The fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4600) was used to monitor the PL spectra of the 7-HC product every 5min using a 342nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure, morphology, and chemical state of Gd-C-TNS photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images of the P25, TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, C-3-TNSs, and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs specimens are shown in Fig. S1. As seen in the figure (S1a–S1b), granules with a size of dozens of nanometers are almost mono-dispersive, and the original TiO2 products have a granular morphology. Uniform sheet-like clusters of nanosheets are formed (Fig. S1c) after the hydrothermal treatment at 130°C for 3h, which is consistent with the results of previous studies [25,38]. The formation of TNSs is ascribed to the exfoliation of granular TiO2 into sheet-like structures under alkaline hydrothermal conditions. During the reaction, numerous TiO bonds of P25 are broken, causing the splitting of TiO2 nanoparticles. Consequently, the TiO2 nanoparticles are stripped into smaller planar nanosheets. Finally, these smaller planar nanosheets join together to form larger planar nanosheets. SEM images of 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, C-3-TNSs, and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs are shown in Fig. S1d–f, respectively. After the introduction of gadolinium and/or the carbon source, the nanosheet structure is not destroyed during the hydrothermal treatment. In addition, the TiO2 nanosheets are thinner (∼3.6nm) than the raw TiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. S1g and h, Supporting information). A decrease in the diffusion path of the photo-generated carriers can be ascribed to the thinness of the nanosheets. This can help improve the separation rate of photo-induced carriers.

TEM images of the corresponding SAED patterns and high-resolution TEM images of the samples are shown in Fig. 1 a–j. Granular crystals with diameters of 20–40nm can be seen in the Degussa P25 (Fig. 1a and b). The SAED patterns indicate the phases of polycrystalline, including (2 1 1) and (1 1 2) diffractions of the rutile phase and (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (2 0 4), and (2 1 5) diffractions of the anatase phase. Fig. 1c and d shows the sheet-like structures of the samples formed after 3h of hydrothermal treatment. Comparing the SAED patterns of P25 and TNSs, new diffraction rings with a lower contrast for TNSs are observed, and the initial diffraction rings have completely disappeared, indicating a poor crystallinity and the transformation of the crystal structure of the TNSs (Fig. 1d). Nanosheets with a width of approximately several tens of nanometers and a length of more than 100nm were mainly included in the samples. Based on the increase in the nanosheet dimensions, small nanosheets formed from the granular crystals because of the oxolation of the OH species at the edge of the raw nanosheets [39,40]. Alvarez–Ramirez et al. suggested that P25 TiO2 powders react with an NaOH solution to form highly disordered TiO2-based nanosheets under hydrothermal conditions (130°C, 3h), and TiO2 nanoparticles are stripped into thin lamellar fragments [41]. After the process of stripping to a certain extent, numerous TiO bonds are broken to form TiO2 nanosheets. The transformation from TiO2 nanoparticles (3D) to TiO2 nanosheets (2D) is gradual; it proceeds from the surface region and then extends to the bulk. Initially, the TiO bonds on the surface are broken. During the breaking process for the TiO bonds, the O atoms are twisted or rotated, creating an angle between the Ti and O atoms. This can lead to the formation of lepidocrocite-type layers, which have been suggested to be the construction units in any layered titanate. Then, the thin lamellar fragments link with each other to form larger planar nanosheets.

Fig. 1e–j shows the SAED patterns and high-resolution TEM images of the 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, C-3-TNSs, and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs specimens, respectively. The samples do not exhibit well-resolved lattice fringes or other typical crystal characteristics compared with P25, indicating their relatively low crystallinity. However, the bare TNSs possess a similar sheet-like structure (Fig. 1e, g, and i). The surface of the 0.5%Gd-C-0- TNSs (Fig. 1f) is rougher than that of bare TNSs after the introduction of the gadolinium source (Gd/Ti=0.5 at%). Small particles (about 1–2nm) appear on the TNSs surface, which suggests the formation of loaded gadolinium oxides. When using a glucose solution of concentration 3.0g/L, the surface of the C-3-TNSs is much rougher than even that of the TNSs and 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs (Fig. 1h). Small particles of 2–3nm are formed on the surface of the TNSs, indicating the successful deposition of CDs. This can be ascribed to the carbonization and condensation of glucose molecules during the second phase of the hydrothermal reaction, which leads to a rough surface for the C-TNSs. When both the gadolinium source and glucose solution are introduced together, more crowded nanoparticles (Gd2O3 nanoparticles & CDs) are formed on the TNS surface (Fig. 1j).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected to further confirm the structures of the P25, TNS, 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNS, and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNS samples. Two bands (Fig. S3, Supporting information) at around 3300cm−1 and 1630cm−1 in the P25 spectrum were ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH and bending vibration of HOH, respectively [25,39]. The intensity of the band (at around 3300cm−1) related to the hydroxyl (OH) groups for the samples of TNSs, 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs was higher than that of P25, indicating the abundance OH groups on the surface of the 2D nanosheets. The surface OH group plays a key role in its photocatalytic performance and adsorption because the groups of surface OH can attract pollutants and capture holes to create the radicals of Ti–OH+ [25,39,40]. Thus, the enhanced adsorption and photocatalytic activity of the 2D sheet-like samples may be ascribed to the high concentration of OH groups. The absorption bands that are observed at 3340cm−1 and 1651cm−1 for the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO, respectively [43]. Moreover, the peak at 1452cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs is attributed to the coordination between the COO− and Ti center, indicating that the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs consist of CDs and TNSs [45].

Wide angle X-ray diffraction patterns were used to identify the crystallization of the P25, TNS, @Gd-C-0-TNS (@=0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%, and 2.0%), and 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNS (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0) samples. Fig. 2 a displays a mischcrystal that includes the anatase phase (JCPDS No. 89-4921) and rutile phase (JCPDS No. 89-0552) for P25. A comparison of the spectra of TNSs and P25 shows that the original peaks of P25 considerably decreased, which indicates that the TNSs are not well crystallized after the alkaline hydrothermal treatment. According to previous reports [46,47], sodium titanates were formed from high-crystalline titania powder under hydrothermal treatment. The sodium titanate was converted to hydrogen titanate after being washed with an HCl solution, and exhibited poor crystalline features. In addition, some distinctive peaks disappeared, indicating a conversion of the 3D P25 to 2D TNSs in the hydrothermal reaction. Furthermore, the XRD patterns of @Gd-C-0-TNSs (@=0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%, and 2.0%) that contained different ratios of Gd/Ti were similar. However, as the Gd2O3 nanoparticles increased, the intensities of the R(1 1 0), A(1 0 5), A(1 0 1), and A(2 0 0) patterns slowly increased, and the related full width at half maximum (FWHM) values became more narrow. These results indicate that the transformation from P25 (3D) to TNSs (2D) may be slightly affected by introducing a gadolinium source. Further, the characteristic peaks of gadolinium oxide were not found in the XRD pattern. In fact, there are two possibilities for why gadolinium oxide was not observed: amorphous gadolinium oxide might not have been developed or no gadolinium oxide existed in the product. Further details are provided in the Supplementary information (Fig. S4). It can be found that the introduced gadolinium was primarily confined to the surface of the TNSs in the form of amorphous oxides (Gd2O3). The XRD peaks (Fig. 2b) of the 0.5%Gd-C-XTNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0) were almost the same as that of pure TNSs, indicating that the modification of CDs did not affect the structure of the samples (0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs). No diffraction peaks related to CDs were found in the XRD pattern, which may be attributed to their poor crystallinity [31,33].

Fig. 2c presents the Raman spectra of the P25, pure TNSs, and @Gd-C-0-TNSs (@=0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%, and 2.0%) in the range of 100–800cm−1. The peaks at around 147cm−1 (Eg), 197cm−1 (Eg), 399cm−1 (Eg), and 643cm−1 (Eg) are the characteristic peaks of the anatase phase [48,49]. It is found that hydrothermal treatment (10M, 130°C) leads to poorer intensities for all the characteristic peaks of the spectrum for the TNSs. Two new peaks appear at around 279cm−1 (B1g) and 456cm−1 (Eg), which are ascribed to titanate [50]. The sodium titanates may initially be formed from raw titania powder through the hydrothermal treatment; the formed sodium titanate products were subsequently converted to titanate through washing with a 0.1M solution of HCl for 3h [25,46]. Meanwhile, a large number of OH groups may be generated on the surface of the nanosheets through an ion exchange process, which can effectively enhance the adsorption of pollutants onto adsorbents. The Raman spectra of the @Gd-C-0-TNSs (@=0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%, and 2.0%) are shown in Fig. 2c. The major Eg mode (∼147cm−1) is slowly enhanced by an increase in the number of Gd2O3 nanoparticles (@Gd=0.0 at% to 2.0 at%). The intensity of the Eg mode (∼147cm−1) for 2.0%Gd-C-0-TNSs is 6.23-fold greater than that of pure TNSs, suggesting that the transformation from P25 (3D) to TNSs (2D) may be affected by the loaded Gd2O3 nanoparticles. Gaussian and Lorentzian multi-peak fitting was used to analyze the Raman spectra. The main Eg mode (∼147cm−1) showed a weak frequency shift (the inset of Fig. 4c) and its FWHM decreased from 13.21cm−1 to 11.41cm−1 with the amount of Gd2O3 nanoparticles, suggesting that the loaded Gd2O3 may influence the Ti–O lattice vibration. The Raman spectra of the 0.5%Gd-C-XTNSs (X=1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0) were almost the same as those of the 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0), as shown in Fig. 2d. The results are consistent with those of XRD studies.

The chemical status and surface composites of the specimens were studied using XPS. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3
a) showed the characteristic peaks of Ti, O, Gd, and C elements. Fig. 3b shows the broad and asymmetric O 1s core level spectra of the TNSs, which are fitted by two Gaussian peaks at about 531.58eV (hydroxyl oxygen) and 530.02eV (TiO) [51,52]. The O 1s core level spectra of the 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs showed a shift of 0.11eV, suggesting the formation of TiOGd bonds [28]. Thus, the loaded Gd2O3 is presented on the surface of the TNSs through TiOGd bonds at the interface, which is in good agreement with the results of earlier studies [28,53]. Compared with the O 1s spectra of the 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs, no obvious shift was found in the case of the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs. From the peak fitting, it is clear that the O 1s area of pure TNSs includes 82.36% TiO bonds and 17.64% hydroxyl oxygen (the O 1s region of the 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs contains 80.73% TiO bonds and 19.27% hydroxyl oxygen), whereas the O 1s region of the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs contains 77.48% TiO bonds and 22.52% hydroxyl oxygen. The surface hydroxyl group of the photocatalyst plays a key role in its adsorption and photocatalytic performance, and more hydroxyl groups increase the photoactivity [25,40]. The XPS spectra of the Ti 2p region are shown in Fig. 3c. The peaks of Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 are located at ∼458eV and ∼464eV, respectively. Because of the formation of TiOGd bonds, the Ti 2p peak spectra of the 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs showed a shift of 0.39eV to a higher BE with an increase in the amount of Gd2O3 nanoparticles. The region of Ti 2p was decomposed into two regions of Ti4+ (464.05eV and 458.30eV) and Ti3+ (463.30eV and 457.55eV) [54,55]. These peaks were used to estimate the concentrations of Ti4+ and Ti3+. It was observed that the concentration of Ti3+ gradually increased (Table 2 ) with the amount of Gd2O3. In general, Ti3+ is considered to be a sound source of photocatalytic performance. It is speculated that a reduction of Ti4+ occurred during the development and de-lamination procedure for the Gd-TNSs. The addition of gadolinium might have affected the de-lamination of P25 and the development of nanosheets. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the Gd 4d regions for the 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs and 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs exhibited a peak corresponding to the Gd at 142.03eV (Fig. 3d). The XPS spectra showed the C 1s region for the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs (Fig. 3e), which originated from the carbon contaminants that were absorbed, and was used for correcting the binding energy [56] as an internal standard. The spectra were studied by conducting a Shirley background subtraction and were fitted by the XPS Peak 4.1 software. The peaks evolved at 288.4eV and 286.4eV were assigned to CO and COH, respectively, and the major contribution at 284.6eV was attributed to Cn [57]. The concentration of Cn for the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs, which was measured using the area of the corresponding peak, was 73.58%. This suggested that the deposited carbon was mainly presented in the form of Cn, with a small contribution from CO and COH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of two different preparation processes for Gd-C-TNSs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 2 (a) displays the crystal structures of the P25 (anatase), Na2Ti3O7 along the [001] axis, and H2Ti3O7 along the [001] axis. The TiO2 nanoparticles (3D) initially dissolve, later strip to sheet-like Na2Ti3O7, and finally become TNSs (2D) during the hydrothermal reaction [58–60]. From the 10mol/L NaOH solution, Na+ ions are contained in the product of this step (Na-TNSs). Then, the Na+ ions can exchange protons by adding the HCl solution [25,27]. The proton exchange is important for the samples to increase the photocatalytic activity. Na-TNSs are more suitable than TNSs for the deposition of CDs [61,62]. For the deposition of CDs, choosing TNSs as the substrate (method I in Scheme 2 (b)) will damage the nanosheet structure. Hence, Na-TNSs were selected as a substrate for the preparation of C-TNSs (method II in Scheme 2(b)). During the magnetic stirring process, the glucose molecules are absorbed by the Na-TNSs. The absorbed glucose is turned into phenol, acid, aldehyde, etc. when the mixture is subjected to high pressure and high temperature. The organic molecules polymerize, and experience the processes of nucleation, carbonization, and condensation to form CDs [63,64]. The as-prepared Gd-C-Na-TNSs were proton exchanged by adding a 0.1mol/L solution of HCl to form Gd-C-TNSs after the deposition of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectric behavior of Gd-C-TNS photocatalyst and pure TNSs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0). As seen in this figure, the optical absorption gradually rose in the visible region with the amount of CDs, indicating the presence of surface-absorbed CDs on the specimens and the release of more photo-induced carriers than that of pure TNSs from the Gd-C-TNSs under the same illumination and suggesting that the visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity could be improved. The variation in optical absorption is intuitively reflected by the color difference of the samples, which show a change from white to gray, and then to brown, with increases in the amount of CDs. Furthermore, a slight red shift is present in the DRS, and the maximum absorbance of light is detected for the 0.5%Gd-C-10-TNSs with an increase in the amount of modified CDs nanoparticles (from 0.0g/L to 10.0g/L). This can be attributed to the charge transfer between the modified CDs and the conduction (or valence) band of the TNSs. The band gaps (Eg) were estimated according to the Kubelka–Munk function, using a suitable equation ((αhν)1/2 =A (hν−Eg)) for indirectly transit band-gap, where hν, α, A, and Eg stand for the photon energy, absorption coefficient, constant, and band gap, respectively [65]. The x intercept of the tangents was used to estimate the Eg values of the specimens, and the obtained band-gap values are listed in Table 1. The band-gaps of the samples decreased from 3.17eV to 2.87eV with the content of CDs (from 0.0g/L to 10.0g/L).

In order to evaluate the recombination efficiency of the photo-generated carriers, the fluorescence spectra (FL) of all the samples were obtained using an excitation wavelength of 300nm. Under unified conditions, because the recombination of excited photo-generated carriers mainly leads to FL emission, a lower FL intensity shows a lower recombination efficiency for the photo-generated carriers. The FL intensities of the @-Gd-TNSs (@=0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 0.75%, 1.0%, and 1.5%) were measured. The minimum was obtained when the loading ratio was 0.5 at%, indicating that 0.5 at% is the optimal loading ratio (Fig. S5, Supporting information). Fig. 4b shows the FL spectra of the pure TNSs and 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0). As shown in Fig. 4b, four characteristic peaks at 451nm, 468nm, 483nm, and 493nm appear in the FL spectra. These four FL signals were due to excitonic FL, which mainly resulted from surface oxygen vacancies and defects in the samples. The FL peaks at 451 and 468nm are attributed to band edge free exactions, and the other two peaks at 483 and 493nm are attributed to bound excitons [28,66]. The FL intensity was suppressed by increasing the amount of modified CDs, which implied that the surface-absorbed CDs acted as electron-traps and effectively promoted the separation of photo-generated charges. In addition, the fluorescence intensity decreased with a small amount of CDs (≤3.0g/L). Nonetheless, the fluorescence intensity of the specimens was higher than that of pure TNSs when the loaded amount reached 10.0g/L. The 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs had the minimal electron–hole pair recombination efficiency (Fig. 4b), indicating that the optimum modification amount was 3.0g/L.

A surface photocurrent (SPC) experiment was also conducted. Fig. 4c shows the photocurrent versus time (I–t) curves of the samples, which represent the role of CDs in improving the efficiency of photo-generated carrier separation. The photocurrents of the samples had the following relations: pure TNSs<0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs<0.5%Gd-C-1-TNSs<0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs>0.5%Gd-C-6-TNSs>0.5%Gd-C-10-TNSs. The samples had comparable light absorption at<400nm (UV-vis spectra in Fig. 4a), which suggested an efficient separation of photo-generated carriers and was attributable to the appropriate amount of CDs (3.0g/L). The efficiency of the photo-generated charge separation gradually increased and then decreased with the increasing amount of CDs, reaching the highest value for 3.0g/L of CDs. In addition, after six light-on and -off cycles, no obvious decay was found in the SPC spectra for the Gd-C-TNSs samples. Thus, the separation and recombination of carriers remained in a stable state. Therefore, the improvement in the photocatalytic activities exhibited by the Gd-C-TNSs samples was mainly attributed to the continuous and effective separation of carriers.

The EIS spectra are used to further analyze the charge transfer behavior of the samples. The classic Nyquist plots of the pure TNSs and 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0) are shown in Fig. 4d. The contributions from the constant phase element and charge-transfer resistance to the electrolyte/photocatalyst interface result in the semicircle in the EIS spectra [67,68]. As seen in Fig. 4d, the semicircle of the 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs is much smaller than that of the TNSs, which indicates a remarkable decrease in the charge transfer resistance with the modification with a small amount of CDs. The 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs exhibit the smallest semicircle, implying that 3.0g/L is the optimal loading amount that facilitates the fastest charge transfer rate. The enhanced charge transfer resistance for the 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (x≥3.0g/L) can be attributed to the excess CDs, which may act as new recombination centers for the photo-induced carriers. This synthesis method offers good conditions for interactions to take place among the TNSs, Gd2O3 nanoparticles, and carbon dots. The formed architectures are converted into interfacial fields and lead to an efficient separation of photo-induced charges. Thus, appropriate modification quantities (Gd/Ti=0.5 at% and CDs=3.0g/L) are considered to be one of the major reasons for the improved rate of charge carrier transfer, which provides a higher visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic effects of Gd-C-TNS samples in single Cr(VI) solution
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic decrease in Cr(VI) in a K2Cr2O7 aqueous solution as an example of toxic heavy metal pollution was used to assess the photocatalytic activity of the specimens under visible light irradiation. Initially, photocatalytic reductions of Cr(VI) at different pH values for the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs were performed, and the results are shown in Fig. S7. Apparently, the acid medium could promote the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) because of the existence of abundant H+. The rate of reaction reached a maximum of 12.069×10−3·min−1 at pH=1, while it was 1.27×10−3·min−1 for pH=9. The photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) was noticeably enhanced by a decrease in the pH. Thus, at lower pH, a higher Cr(VI) removal efficiency would be expected. In addition, the degradation rates and photocatalytic efficiencies at pH=1 and pH=3 were slightly different. Therefore, various 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0) were evaluated at pH=3. In addition, the calculated PZC of the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs was 6.62, as shown in Fig. S8. Therefore, the Gd-C-TNS photocatalyst was positively charged at pH values of no more than 6.63, which facilitated the Cr(VI) adsorption (i.e., Cr2O7 2−or CrO4 2−). In contrast, when the pH reached 6.63, the Cr(VI) adsorption decreased.

Fig. 5 shows the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and reaction kinetic equation over the TNS and 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNS (x=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0) specimens under visible light irradiation at pH=3. A blank experiment was carried out in the absence of a photocatalyst for a comparison, and the concentration of Cr(VI) remained consistent, indicating that the photolysis reaction was negligible, and K2Cr2O7 is relatively stable. No considerable reduction in the amount of Cr(VI) for the TNSs was found after being exposed to visible irradiation for 120min, implying that there was no visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity for Cr(VI) in pure TNSs. Conversely, a higher photocatalytic efficiency was found in the 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNS (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0) samples. Thus, the photocatalytic decrease in Cr(VI) was ascribed to the modified CDs. With an increase in the amount of modified CDs, the visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity of the 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs greatly increased and reached a maximum for 3.0g/L of CDs. However, the photocatalytic efficiency slowly decreased when the concentration of CDs became higher than 3.0g/L. A kinetic simulation was conducted. According to the results, the following first-order relationship was obeyed by the reactions: −ln (C t/C 0)= K app t, where C 0 is the initial concentration solution, C t is the solution concentration at reaction time t, K app is the first-order coefficient, and t is the reaction time [69]. The reaction rate values were obtained using polynomial fitting (Fig. 5c and d). The reaction rate constant (K app) of the photocatalytic degradation for the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs (0.01207min−1) was 6.208-fold that of the TNSs (0.00194min−1) (Table 3 ).

In addition, electrolyte effects were also examined using NaCl, CaCl2, and Na2SO4 at 5mM, and the Cr(VI) removal effectiveness is shown in Fig. S9. The addition of NaCl did not affect the photocatalytic decrease in Cr(VI) in comparison to the system without any electrolyte. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic decrease in Cr(VI) was enhanced by the addition of CaCl2. On the other hand, the photo-reduction of Cr(VI) may have been inhibited by the addition of Na2SO4. The results were in agreement with previous reports [71–74]. Photocatalysis was promoted by the addition of Ca2+ by virtue of manifested ionic bridging functions in the form of TiOH+-Cr(VI)–Ca2+-Cr(VI) linkages, whereas the photo-decrease in Cr(VI) could be inhibited by SO4 2−, which showed the synergy of the adsorption and photocatalysis even at a higher ionic electrolyte strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic effect of Gd-C-TNSs samples in isolated RhB solution
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation of RhB was conducted in an isolated RhB system (20ppm) for comparison to analyze the photocatalytic efficiency of the simultaneous decontamination of RhB and Cr(VI) in an Cr(VI)–RhB composite system. The photocatalytic effects of nanoparticles, @-Gd-TNSs (@=0.1%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 0.75%, 1.0%, and 1.5%), were measured, which showed that the best photocatalytic activity was obtained by 0.5 at%, indicating that 0.5 at% is the optimal ratio (Fig. S6, Supporting information). Fig. 6 shows the photocatalytic degradation of the 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs (X=0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 6.0, and 10.0). Low visible-light-induced photocatalytic activities for RhB were shown by bare TNSs. After the modification by an appropriate amount of CDs, the photocatalytic activity was remarkably improved (Fig. 6b). Obviously, the rise of loaded CDs led to an increase in the visible-light-induced photocatalytic activities when the loaded amount of CDs was low (≤3.0g/L). The photocatalytic activity of the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs reached the maximum, which suggested that the optimal ratio of CDs was 3.0g/L. Then, the photocatalytic activities of the specimens slowly decreased with further increases in the amount of CDs. The photocatalytic activity of the 0.5%Gd-C-10-TNSs was lower than that of pure TNSs. Fig. 6c–d shows that the experimental data confirm the first-order kinetic equation. It is found that the visible-light-induced reaction rate (Kapp) of the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs (49.796min−1) is 11.66-fold that of the TNSs (4.272min−1) and 8.08-fold that of the 0.5%Gd-C-0-TNSs (6.163min−1), as listed in Table 3. Therefore, it can be noted that the photocatalytic activity of the specimens was effectively enhanced by an appropriate amount of CDs (≤3.0g/L); too many CDs were harmful when the loaded amount was higher than the optimal value of 3.0g/L. The results for the photocatalytic activity confirmed those shown by the SPC spectra and FL spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic effect of simultaneous decontamination of Cr(VI) and RhB in Cr(VI)–RhB coexistence system
ce:section.ce:para: An ICP emission spectrometer was also used to measure the concentration of residual Cr ions in the specimens after photocatalytic treatment using the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNS sample (Fig. 7c). A total residual Cr(VI) ion concentration of 2.143ppm was measured after 120min of treatment. However, the measured residual Cr(VI) ion concentration (0.0392ppm) was found to be lower than that of industrial wastewater (0.25ppm) and drinking water (0.05ppm) after the same treatment time (120min) in the Cr(VI)–RhB composite system. The surface absorption of Cr in the specimens was evaluated using the XPS (Fig. S10) after the photocatalytic reaction in the presence of the Cr(VI)–RhB composite system and isolated Cr(VI) system. The broad peak for Cr 2p3/2 was fitted to numerous peaks with various BE values. The other two sub-peaks at around 586.03 and 579.52eV corresponded to the characteristic binding energies of the adsorbed Cr(VI). The three major peaks at 578.62, 576.32, and 576.24eV were attributed to the hydroxide forms of Cr(III) (such as Cr(OH)3 and Cr(III)xOy) [76,77]. The synergistic photocatalysis at the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs could remove Cr ions through simultaneous adsorption, Cr(VI) reduction, and RhB oxidation. In addition, total organic carbon (TOC) experiments with 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs were performed to determine the extent of the mineralization of the RhB solution. As shown in Fig. 7d, for the isolated system of aqueous RhB, the TOC concentration of the RhB solution (20mg/L) decreased from 12.35mg/L to 3.28mg/L after 70min of visible-light irradiation. The TOC concentration of the RhB solution (20mg/L) decreased from 12.35mg/L to 0.16mg/L under visible light irradiation for 70min for the Cr(VI)–RhB composite system, which indicated that the extent of mineralization of the RhB molecules was considerably improved by the presence of Cr(VI). The mineralization of the isolated RhB system increased from 73.4% to 98.7% when it became the Cr(VI)–RhB composite system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism to enhance visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity for Gd2O3 nanoparticle and carbon dot co-modified samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further distinguish the influence of modified CDs, the formation rate of OH radicals was investigated by monitoring the generation of 7-hydroxycoumarin. The OH radicals are generated from the oxidation of H2O molecules or OH− ions by the electron-induced, photo-based, multi-step decrease of O2 − radicals, or the photo-generated holes (h+) are the main active species accountable for the photocatalytic reaction [78,79]. The changes in the fluorescence intensity recorded against the illumination time for 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNSs and TNSs are shown in Fig. 8 , which illustrates that the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the amount of produced photo-induced OH radicals. The gradual and significant rise in the fluorescence intensity for the 0.5%Gd-C-X-TNS specimens indicated that the hydroxyl radical formation was proportional to the irradiation time, whereas the intensity increase was almost negligible and too slow in the case of TNSs, implying the poor formation of OH radicals under visible light irradiation. Compared with the pure TNS sample, specimens modified by a small amount of CDs demonstrated an enhanced rate after illumination for 30min. The enhancement of the OH radical generation could significantly improve the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the formation rate of OH radicals first increased and then decreased with an increase in the amount of CDs. The highest rate of OH radical formation was obtained for the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs, which was 9.41-fold that of pure TNSs. The results were in agreement with those of the photocatalytic degradation.

The transfer and separation of charge carriers among the carbon dots, loaded Gd2O3 nanoparticles, and TiO2-oriented nanosheets are illustrated in Scheme 3 . The formation of a micro-heterostructure among the carbon dots, Gd2O3 nanoparticles, and TiO2-oriented nanosheets is the key factor for the photoactivity enhancement. The transfer and separation of the charge carriers of the photocatalysts for the Gd-C-TNSs were expected to be performed during the simultaneous Cr(VI) and RhB decontamination in the Cr(VI)–RhB composite system under visible light irradiation. The excitation of carbon dots, which produced charge carriers, was followed by electron injection from excited carbon dots to the conduction band (CB) of the TNSs. Thus, the recombination of photo-induced electron–hole pairs could effectively be suppressed.

Scheme 3 shows the transfer and charge separation for the Gd-C-TNSs during the Cr(VI) reduction process under visible light irradiation in the isolated Cr(VI) system. Initially, in order to generate photo-generated carriers, carbon dots were stimulated by visible light irradiation, followed by electron injection from excited carbon dots into the CB of the TNSs (or Gd2O3 nanoparticles) for the Cr(VI) removal in the reaction systems. Then, the absorbed Cr(VI) of the sample was reduced to Cr(III) by electrons, and the H2O accepted holes in the absence of reducing agents. Thus, the photocatalysis redox cycle continued. A portion of Cr(VI) was adsorbed to the carbon dots and Gd2O3 nanoparticles by the OH groups during the photocatalysis process. However, during the adsorption process, the Cr(III) ions were mainly absorbed by the TNSs. For the Gd-C-TNSs, the Cr removal process was different from that of bare TNSs because of the simultaneous occurrence of adsorption and photocatalysis in the Gd-C-TNSs. In this system, the synergistic effects could be recognized in relation to the coordination among the Gd2O3 nanoparticles, carbon dots, and TNSs. Further, because of their fast kinetics and high adsorption capacity, Cr(III) was adsorbed by the TNSs more quickly, and the surface Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III) by photo-induced electrons. The transfer of Cr(III) from the TNSs to carbon dots or Gd2O3 led to an exposure to Cr(VI) and the continuous release of the abundant photocatalytic sites of the Gd2O3 (or carbon dots). Thus, the photocatalytic activity was enhanced. During the removal Cr(VI) process, a photocatalytic decrease in Cr(VI) could be promoted with numerous receivers of Cr(III) by the cooperation between carbon dots, TNSs, and Gd2O3 nanoparticles through the adsorption of TNSs. It is worth mentioning that Cr(III) served as an inhibitor. Furthermore, Cr(III) entered the TNSs through an ion-exchange mechanism, with H+ at the inner layers of the TNSs. The protons could effectively limit the recombination of holes and electrons. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of the specimen was promoted. On the other hand, a donor state (2.6eV) formed in the forbidden band of the TNSs when Cr(III) entered the TNSs at a later stage. As a result, the photocatalytic capability of the specimen could be enhanced, and the Gd-C-TNSs-Cr(III) was allowed to remove the Cr(VI) [80,81]. The Cr(III) absorbance could greatly enhance the efficiency of the photo-generated carrier separation and increase the UV–vis adsorption of the specimens. At the donor level, a photo-generated electron (e−) would leave a hole (h+) behind and transfer to the CB of the TNSs when a photon was captured by Cr(III) from the internal layers of the Gd-C-TNSs. At the same time, OH− reacted with h+ in the solution and served as an h+ scavenger. Thus, the formation of a donor and effective separation of the charges could greatly enhance the photocatalysis of Gd-C-TNSs-Cr(III).

The synergistic increase in the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of RhB for the Gd-C-TNSs in the Cr(VI)-RhB composite system is shown in Scheme 3. Thus, as a strong oxidant, Cr(VI) can greatly consume photocatalytically generated electrons at the CD of specimens in the Cr(VI)–RhB coexistence system. Simultaneously, as an oxidisable organic substance, RhB consumed the photocatalytically generated holes at the VD, which resulted in an efficient suppression of the recombination of charge carriers and a dramatic enhancement in photocatalytic efficiency in comparison to the single systems. During the hole capture of RhB at the VD, the consumption of electrons by Cr(VI) at CD increased the separation efficiency of the photo-generated carriers and thus improved the photocatalytic degradation of RhB molecules and photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction efficiency.

The redox potential of carbon* is very low. Thus, it is not easily oxidized by Cr(VI) and is insufficient to oxidize water [82]. Therefore, in HOMO, the carbon* holes cannot be refilled without another electron donor, leading to the eventual decomposition of carbon species in smaller organic compounds. A similar process was described in a previous report on the self-photosensitized oxidative transformation of RhB in an aqueous TiO2 dispersion under visible light irradiation [83]. During the photocatalytic process, a TOC determination of the reactive solution was used in recent studies to show the decomposition of carbon species.

Fig. S11 describes a gradual increase in the concentration of TOC to ∼1.26mg/L in the presence of Cr(VI) during 70min of photocatalytic reaction. A control experiment was conducted in ID water without Cr(VI) and a photocatalyst, which meant that the small amount of TOC could be ignored. Because carbon species were the only source of TOC, the carbon species were dissolved into the reaction solution and experienced photodecomposition.

The addition of RhB could protect the sensitized CDs from decomposition in light by the photocatalytic mechanism, as previously mentioned. Accordingly, after the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) with and without the addition of RhB (Fig. S12, Supporting information), the FT-IR spectrum of the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs was evaluated. After the Cr(VI) reduction, the FT-IR spectra of 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs showed the disappearance of the carbon band, which reflected the degradation of carbon species from the catalyst. In contrast, in the photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction system, the spectra were not affected by the presence of RhB. These results demonstrated the synergism between the reduced metal ions and oxidized organic matter in the Cr(VI)–RhB composite system.

Therefore, it could be found that during the removal process for Cr(VI), carbon species were slowly decomposed. Yet, this could be prevented by the addition of RhB. Therefore, when employed in conjunction with oxidizable organic matter, Gd-C-TNSs should have a longer durability. In order to test this hypothesis, the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs were reused ten times in the Cr(VI)–RhB composite system and isolated Cr(VI) reaction system.

For the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNS samples, the photocatalytic performances dramatically decreased in the isolated Cr(VI) system with repeated usage (Fig. 9 a). The removal efficiency for Cr(VI) dropped from 78.12% to 40.16% after 10 uses, indicating that carbon species had been lost, with a decrease in the sensitizing ability. However, the photocatalytic efficiency slightly decreased in the Cr(VI)–RhB composite system, as shown in Fig. 9b. The degradation efficiency for RhB and the removal of Cr(VI) were maintained at more than 83.59% and 93.38%, respectively, after the 10th use for the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs. The occurrence of synergism between the Cr(VI) reduction and RhB oxidation in the photocatalytic system was also confirmed by the results. In addition, after cyclic usage, the recovery of photocatalysts becomes an important practical problem. It was found that the aqueous suspensions of Degussa P25 powder were turbid after several hours, whereas the Gd-C-TNSs could be readily separated from the aqueous solution through a facile sedimentation process within not more than 1h (Fig. S13, Supporting information). The specimens could be reused because of their easy recovery characteristic.

TiO2-based nanosheets with highly dispersed Gd2O3 nanoparticles (1–2nm) and carbon dots (2–3nm) were synthesized using a novel two-pot hydrothermal approach. Sheet-like structures with a length of more than 100nm, width of 30–100nm, small thickness of approximately 3.6nm, and higher specific surface area of 240–350m2/g were formed in the specimens. The crystallization of the samples gradually increased with the amount of Gd2O3 nanoparticles, which was confirmed by the XRD, BET, and Raman spectra, and the amount of TiO2 slowly increased in the transformation process from 3D to 2D. The modification of the CDs resulted in a shift in the absorption edge toward a longer wavelength and a reduction in the band-gap from 3.17eV to 2.87eV with an increase in the number of CDs (from 0.0g/L to 10.0g/L). The FL intensity first decreased and later increased with an increased in the amount of CDs, with a crossover at the 0.5%Gd-C-3-TNSs. The SPC and FL spectra showed that the appropriate amount of Gd2O3 nanoparticles and CDs (Gd/Ti=0.5 at% and content of CDs=3.0g/L) could decrease the recombination rate of the charge carriers, which improved the charge separation efficiency. The Gd-C-TNS photocatalysts showed excellent photoactivity for the removal of the Cr(VI) solution. The photocatalytic activity first slowly increased and later decreased with an increase in the amount of CDs, achieving a maximum value for the removal of the aqueous Cr(VI) solution at Gd/Ti=0.5 at% and CDs=3.0g/L. Because of the formation of TiOH+-Cr(VI)–Ca2+-Cr(VI) linkages and an ionic bridging function, photocatalysis was promoted by the addition of Ca2+, whereas the photo-reduction of Cr(VI) was slowly inhibited by SO4 2−. A sound synergism between the adsorption and photocatalysis was observed at a high ionic electrolyte strength. The presence of RhB greatly promoted the photocatalytic decrease in Cr(VI), and the synergism between the degradation of RhB and Cr(VI) reduction was confirmed by evaluating the repeated usage of Gd-C-TNSs and its effect on the photocatalytic efficiency. An alternate scheme was also proposed to enhance the visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity.
